title: Episode 9 Pun
wikitext:
[]: Episode 9 Pun             Quote Post by GiftedPilot » Fri Oct 19, 2012 6:27 pm Just some little NGE wordplay. In episode 9, Asuka states that no girl and boy should live under the same roof at the age of seven. And the only reason this situation is taking place is because the SEVENTH angel is attacking.

[]:             Quote Post by Rj123541 » Fri Oct 19, 2012 7:17 pm Ok...?

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Fri Oct 19, 2012 7:40 pm I assume you intend to also draw some other parallels between the seventh angel and Shinji/Asuka's situation in episode 7?

[]:             Quote Post by Rj123541 » Fri Oct 19, 2012 7:55 pm Dream wrote: I assume you intend to also draw some other parallels between the seventh angel and Shinji/Asuka's situation in episode 7?

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Fri Oct 19, 2012 9:04 pm Rj123541 wrote: That is why I said Ok...?

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Fri Oct 19, 2012 11:06 pm Platinum Booklets Commentaries wrote: Asuka says, "This is the wall of Jericho, never to fall!" of the sliding door that separates the two rooms, but the "Wall of Jericho" is a reference to the Western film It Happened One Night (1934, America). In the movie, a rich runaway girl and an unemployed newspaper reporter end up spending a night in the same room, and they put a blanket as a divider, calling it the "Wall of Jericho." Incidentally, the original "Wall of Jericho" is a castle wall that appears in the Bible. Also, she says, "It is proper that boys and girls sleep apart after the age of seven," but the correct proverb is "it is proper that the boys and girls sit apart after the age of seven." This is a saying in the ancient Chinese Confucian text of The Book of Rites, and the seat refers to a straw mat. In ancient China, sitting on the same mat meant that the two were husband and wife. Is it the genius girl's pride that leads her to want to use difficult sayings, even though she's not supposed to be used to Japanese yet?

[]:             Quote Post by Rj123541 » Fri Oct 19, 2012 11:55 pm [quote=" Sailor Star Dust":us5xnrzq][/quote:us5xnrzq]Thank you SSD, but it doesn't really look like a pun.

[]:             Quote Post by Blue Monday » Sat Oct 20, 2012 12:27 am

[]:             Quote Post by Rj123541 » Sat Oct 20, 2012 2:58 am Blue Monday wrote:

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Sat Oct 20, 2012 4:03 am Asuka says, "This is the wall of Jericho, never to fall!" of the sliding door that separates the two rooms, but the "Wall of Jericho" is a reference to the Western film It Happened One Night (1934, America). In the movie, a rich runaway girl and an unemployed newspaper reporter end up spending a night in the same room, and they put a blanket as a divider, calling it the "Wall of Jericho." Incidentally, the original "Wall of Jericho" is a castle wall that appears in the Bible. Also, she says, "It is proper that boys and girls sleep apart after the age of seven," but the correct proverb is "it is proper that the boys and girls sit apart after the age of seven." This is a saying in the ancient Chinese Confucian text of The Book of Rites, and the seat refers to a straw mat. In ancient China, sitting on the same mat meant that the two were husband and wife. Is it the genius girl's pride that leads her to want to use difficult sayings, even though she's not supposed to be used to Japanese yet?

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Sat Oct 20, 2012 7:00 am Asuka says, "This is the wall of Jericho, never to fall!" of the sliding door that separates the two rooms, but the "Wall of Jericho" is a reference to the Western film It Happened One Night (1934, America). In the movie, a rich runaway girl and an unemployed newspaper reporter end up spending a night in the same room, and they put a blanket as a divider, calling it the "Wall of Jericho." Incidentally, the original "Wall of Jericho" is a castle wall that appears in the Bible.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Sat Oct 20, 2012 9:31 am CJD wrote: No mention of how the Wall of Jericho actually did fall down? I quite liked the interpretation that Asuka used that wording specifically, hoping Shinji knew the story and could take a hint. I think it was someone here that enlightened me to it...

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sat Oct 20, 2012 9:47 am Rj123541 wrote: Thank you SSD, but it doesn't really look like a pun.

[]:             Quote Post by Quadhonk » Sat Oct 20, 2012 10:05 am Dream wrote: Kind of off-topic, but i never could buy the "Asuka left that as a hint" theory because, well, there's no logical way she could have expected Shinji to know something like that, is there? It's not like she knows for sure he is aware of Jericho's history or anything even remotely like that.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Oct 20, 2012 11:14 am CJD wrote: hoping Shinji knew the story and could take a hint.

[]:             Quote Post by tomrule123 » Sat Oct 20, 2012 11:15 am ... wait, what connection is there between age and a creature? SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sat Oct 20, 2012 2:07 pm I suppose you could tear it apart on a metaphorical level, and possibly even apply a moral intent behind this "pun". Asuka asserts that boys and girls should be separate after the age of seven, even referencing an old proverb. Israfel, the seventh Angel, as we know splits into two separate bodies, possibly representing male and female; the action was even in response to Asuka's "assertion" of force. But, through the progression of the episode, they have to learn to put their gender differences aside and act in unison (something that even the Angel could do) in order to come out victorious in the end. Is this a challenge of traditional gender roles? I don't fucking know.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Sun Oct 21, 2012 10:13 am Dream wrote: Kind of off-topic, but i never could buy the "Asuka left that as a hint" theory because, well, there's no logical way she could have expected Shinji to know something like that, is there? It's not like she knows for sure he is aware of Jericho's history or anything even remotely like that.

[]:             Quote Post by Der Kommissar » Sun Oct 21, 2012 4:04 pm It's not outside the realm of possibility that she subconsciously (or even consciously) sabatoged herself there, either. After all, fear of others is a central theme of Evangelion. Would Asuka even know what to do if Shinji took her up on her flirting aggressively? I have observed in the past that the problem was that Shinji didn't have the horn.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Mon Oct 22, 2012 8:18 am Der Kommissar wrote: It's not outside the realm of possibility that she subconsciously (or even consciously) sabatoged herself there, either. After all, fear of others is a central theme of Evangelion. Would Asuka even know what to do if Shinji took her up on her flirting aggressively?

Article: Episode 9 Pun
 you could tear it apart on a metaphorical level, and possibly even apply a moral intent behind this "pun". Asuka asserts that boys and girls should be separate after the age of seven, even referencing an old proverb. Israfel, the seventh Angel, as we know splits into two separate bodies, possibly representing male and female; the action was even in response to Asuka's "assertion" of force. But, through the progression of the episode, they have to learn to put their gender differences aside and act in unison (something that even the Angel could do) in order to come out victorious in the end. Is this a challenge of traditional gender roles? I don't fucking know.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Sun Oct 21, 2012 10:13 am Dream wrote: Kind of off-topic, but i never could buy the "Asuka left that as a hint" theory because, well, there's no logical way she could have expected Shinji to know something like that, is there? It's not like she knows for sure he is aware of Jericho's history or anything even remotely like that.

[]:             Quote Post by Der Kommissar » Sun Oct 21, 2012 4:04 pm It's not outside the realm of possibility that she subconsciously (or even consciously) sabatoged herself there, either. After all, fear of others is a central theme of Evangelion. Would Asuka even know what to do if Shinji took her up on her flirting aggressively? I have observed in the past that the problem was that Shinji didn't have the horn.

After all, fear of others is a central theme of Evangelion. Would Asuka even know what to do if Shinji took her up on her flirting aggressively?
